title: How to Adjust Glasses at Home: Safe Fixes for Common Fit Issues
description: Glasses crooked, loose, or pinching? Learn which adjustments are safe to do at home and which need a professional. Step-by-step guide for nose pads, temples, and more.
tldr: You can safely adjust nose pads, tighten loose screws, and reposition silicone temple tips at home. Avoid bending metal temples or heating acetate frames — these need professional tools to prevent breakage. Most optical stores adjust glasses free in under five minutes, even if you didn't buy them there.

tldrStats.label: Adjustments you can do at home

definition.text: The process of modifying the fit of eyeglass frames to match the wearer's facial dimensions. Includes bending temples, repositioning nose pads, adjusting bridge width, and correcting lens tilt. Professional adjustments use specialized pliers, frame heaters, and alignment tools.

sections.h2: Adjustments You Can Safely Do at Home
sections.content:
These fixes are low-risk and don't require special tools:

1. Tighten nose pads

If your glasses slide down, the nose pads may have spread apart. Using your fingers (or tweezers for precision), gently press the nose pad arms inward until both pads make full contact with the sides of your nose. Adjust them symmetrically — uneven pads make the frame sit crooked.

2. Tighten hinge screws

Loose hinges make temples wobble and affect fit. Use a glasses-specific screwdriver (included in most cleaning kits) or a precision screwdriver. Turn clockwise to tighten. Don't overtighten — stop when snug. If the screw keeps loosening, a tiny drop of clear nail polish on the threads acts as threadlocker.

3. Reposition silicone temple tips

If your temples have soft silicone covers on the ends, these can slide forward or back to adjust where the frame grips behind your ears. Slide them into a position that feels comfortable without creating pressure points.

These adjustments carry a real risk of breaking your frames if done wrong:

1. Bending metal temples

Metal temples need to curve at precisely the right point behind your ears. An optician uses padded pliers designed for this — household pliers can scratch the finish, bend at the wrong point, or snap thin metal arms. If your temples need reshaping, bring them to your optical store.

2. Reshaping acetate frames

Acetate (plastic) frames become pliable when heated evenly with a specialized frame warmer. Hair dryers heat unevenly and can permanently warp or discolour the frame. Hot water risks melting. An optician heats the frame to the exact right temperature, makes the adjustment, then cools it in the correct position.

3. Adjusting pantoscopic tilt

This is the angle of the lenses relative to your face — it affects how you look through the optical centres. Changing this tilt shifts your visual field and can make your prescription feel wrong. Leave this to your optician, who can verify the tilt with your prescription in mind.

sections.h2: Common Fit Problems and Their Fixes
sections.content:
ProblemLikely CauseDIY FixPro Fix
Glasses slide down noseLoose nose pads or wide bridgeTighten nose pads inwardAdjust temple tension, reshape bridge
Frame sits crookedUneven temple bendCheck nose pads are evenBend temples to match ear heights
Pinching behind earsTemples too short or bent too tightReposition temple tipsExtend temple curve or adjust bend point
Pressure on noseNose pads too tight or wrong bridgeSpread nose pads slightly outwardReplace nose pads, adjust bridge
Lenses seem tiltedUneven pantoscopic tiltDon't attemptAdjust frame front angle
Temples feel loose/wobblyLoose hinge screwsTighten with screwdriverReplace worn screws or hinges

A general rule: if the fix involves moving something soft (silicone pads, rubber tips, screws), it's safe to try at home. If it involves bending, heating, or reshaping the frame material itself, it's a professional job.

A basic glasses toolkit costs $10-$20 and is worth having:

  • Precision screwdriver set: Tiny Phillips and flathead screwdrivers sized for glasses screws. Often included in cleaning kits.
  • Spare screws: Glasses screw kits with assorted sizes are available at pharmacies for under $5. Useful when a screw strips or falls out.
  • Nose pad pliers (optional): Specialized pliers with smooth jaws for adjusting nose pad arms without scratching. Helpful if you adjust pads frequently.
  • Microfiber cloth: Keep lenses clean during adjustments — fingerprints on lenses make it hard to evaluate fit visually.

Skip the hair dryer, regular pliers, or any makeshift heating tool. These cause more damage than they fix.

If you're uncomfortable making adjustments yourself, or if the issue persists after trying these steps, take your glasses to a professional. Optical stores have specialized tools designed specifically for eyewear — household tools like regular pliers can easily crack lenses or snap frame hinges. Most adjustments take less than five minutes in-store and are typically free of charge.

Charm Optical in Edmonton offers free adjustments and minor repairs for any glasses — even if you didn't buy them there. Their opticians can also check whether your frames are still aligned correctly with your prescription, which is especially important for progressive lenses where even small shifts affect vision quality. If your glasses need more than an adjustment, they carry replacement parts for most major brands and can often fix broken hinges, replace nose pads, and resurface temple tips on the spot.

sections.h2: How Often Should Glasses Be Adjusted?
sections.content:
Glasses gradually lose their fit through daily wear — taking them on and off flexes the temples, body heat softens materials, and small impacts shift alignment. A good schedule:

  • Monthly: Quick self-check. Do they sit level? Do they slide? Any pressure points?
  • Every 3-6 months: Professional adjustment. Most optical stores do this free and it takes about five minutes. They'll also clean your glasses with an ultrasonic cleaner and replace worn nose pads.
  • Immediately: If they get sat on, dropped, or bent noticeably. Don't try to fix major bends yourself — bring them in before wearing them, as misaligned lenses can cause headaches.

Regular adjustments extend the life of your frames. A frame that's never adjusted will stretch, warp, and eventually need replacement sooner than one that's maintained. Most Edmonton optical stores welcome walk-ins for quick adjustments.

If you're uncomfortable making adjustments yourself, or if the issue persists after trying these steps, take your glasses to a professional. Optical stores have specialized tools designed specifically for eyewear — household tools like regular pliers can easily crack lenses or snap frame hinges. Most adjustments take less than five minutes in-store and are typically free of charge.

Charm Optical in Edmonton offers free adjustments and minor repairs for any glasses — even if you didn't buy them there. Their opticians can also check whether your frames are still aligned correctly with your prescription, which is especially important for progressive lenses where even small shifts affect vision quality. If your glasses need more than an adjustment, they carry replacement parts for most major brands and can often fix broken hinges, replace nose pads, and resurface temple tips on the spot.

sections.h2: When to Visit Your Optician for Professional Help
sections.content:
While many glasses issues can be handled at home, some problems require professional tools and expertise. Bent metal frames need careful adjustment with pliers designed specifically for eyewear — using household tools risks snapping the frame. Nose pad replacement requires the exact size and shape for your frame model. And any adjustment that changes how the lenses sit in front of your eyes can affect your vision quality.

Most optical stores offer free adjustments for glasses purchased from them. At Charm Optical in Edmonton, the opticians will adjust your frames, replace nose pads, tighten screws, and check your lens alignment at no charge — even if your glasses weren't purchased there. This kind of ongoing service is one of the biggest advantages of buying from a local optical store versus ordering online.

If your lenses are scratched, foggy, or the coatings are peeling, it's time for new lenses rather than a repair. Many lens types are available for same-day glasses at Charm Optical at Charm Optical, and their direct insurance billing means you can use your benefits toward the replacement. book an eye exam online to get your prescription checked while you're there — it may have changed since your last visit.

Your eyes are one of the most important tools you have, and they deserve proper care and correction. The technology behind modern eyewear has advanced remarkably in recent years — from digital freeform progressive lenses that customize the optics to your exact wearing position, to lightweight titanium frames that weigh less than a coin, to anti-reflective coatings that virtually eliminate glare. Understanding these options empowers you to make better choices about your eye health and get glasses that genuinely improve your quality of life rather than just meeting a basic need.

Regular eye exams are the foundation of good vision care. The Canadian Association of Optometrists recommends exams every one to two years for adults and annually for children, seniors over 65, and anyone with diabetes or a family history of eye disease. A comprehensive exam does far more than measure your prescription — your optometrist checks the health of your retina, optic nerve, and blood vessels, catching conditions like glaucoma and macular degeneration in their earliest stages. Many serious eye conditions have no noticeable symptoms until significant damage has occurred, making preventive exams essential.

The best next step is always a professional eye exam. Your optometrist can evaluate your specific needs and recommend the right combination of lenses, coatings, and frames for your lifestyle and budget. In Alberta, most employer insurance plans cover comprehensive eye exams, and children under 18 and adults over 65 receive coverage through Alberta Health.

Charm Optical at 5035 Ellerslie Rd SW in South Edmonton offers thorough eye exams with experienced optometrists, a selection of over 500 designer frames from brands like Ray-Ban, Oakley, and Versace, and same-day glasses for many prescription types. They offer direct insurance billing for most Alberta providers, so you only pay the difference at the counter.

Whether you are buying your first pair of glasses, upgrading to progressive lenses, or just need an adjustment on your current frames, having a trusted local optician makes the process smoother. Book an appointment online — most appointments are available within a few days, and walk-ins are welcome for adjustments and frame browsing.

faqs.q: Can I adjust my glasses with a hair dryer?
faqs.a: Not recommended. Hair dryers heat acetate frames unevenly, which can cause permanent warping, discolouration, or white stress marks. Professional opticians use frame warmers that heat evenly at controlled temperatures. If your acetate frames need reshaping, take them to an optical store.

faqs.q: How do I fix glasses that are crooked on my face?
faqs.a: Check that both nose pads are positioned evenly. If the problem is uneven temple bends (one ear sits higher than the other), this needs professional adjustment — an optician bends the temples at different points to match your ear heights.

faqs.q: Do optical stores charge for adjustments?
faqs.a: Most optical stores adjust glasses for free, even if you didn't purchase them there. It takes about five minutes. Some stores may charge a small fee for replacement parts like nose pads or screws, but the labour is typically complimentary.

faqs.q: How do I tighten glasses that are too loose?
faqs.a: For immediate relief, gently tighten nose pads inward and tighten hinge screws. For a lasting fix, an optician can increase temple tension by adjusting the bend behind your ears. This is a free service at most optical stores and takes about five minutes.

faqs.q: Can an optician fix scratched lenses?
faqs.a: Scratched lenses cannot be repaired — the scratch is in the coating or lens material itself. You'll need new lenses. Many single vision lenses are available same-day at Charm Optical in Edmonton, and most insurance plans cover lens replacement every 1-2 years.

faqs.q: How often should I get my glasses adjusted?
faqs.a: Get a professional adjustment every 3-6 months, or whenever your glasses feel loose, crooked, or uncomfortable. Charm Optical offers free adjustments — just walk in. Regular adjustments extend the life of your frames and keep your vision clear.
